title: AI Agents
lesson:
A plain chatbot does one thing: you send a message, it sends words back, done. An AI agent is different. You give it a goal, and it keeps working on its own — thinking, taking actions, looking at the results, and deciding what to do next — until the goal is met or it gives up. The key word is loop. A chatbot answers once; an agent goes round and round.

The intern analogy

Imagine hiring a capable intern. You don't script their every keystroke. You say "book me a meeting room for Thursday and email the team." The intern checks the calendar, sees the room is taken, books a different one, then writes the email. They used tools (calendar, email), observed what happened (room taken), and adjusted. That cycle of decide → act → observe → decide again is exactly what an agent does. The model is the intern's brain; the tools are their hands.

The agent loop

Under the hood an agent runs a simple repeating cycle, often called the agent loop:

  1. Think — the model reads the goal and the history so far, and decides the next step.
  2. Act — it calls a tool (search the web, run code, query a database). This is Tool Calling and Function Calling — the mechanics of how the model asks your code to do something.
  3. Observe — your code runs the tool and feeds the result back into the conversation.
  4. Repeat — the model reads that result and either takes another step or declares the goal done.

This think-act-observe pattern is sometimes called ReAct (Reasoning + Acting). The crucial bit: the model can't do anything itself — it only outputs text. Your program is the muscle that actually runs the tools and loops back. Without that surrounding loop, you don't have an agent; you have a model writing about what it would do.

# The bare-bones agent loop
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Tokyo, in Fahrenheit?"}]
for _ in range(10):                      # max 10 steps, so it can't run forever
    reply = llm.chat(messages, tools=TOOLS)
    if reply.tool_call:                  # model wants to ACT
        result = run_tool(reply.tool_call)   # your code does the work
        messages.append(reply)               # record the request
        messages.append(result)              # OBSERVE: feed result back
    else:
        print(reply.content)             # no tool needed -> final answer
        break

Notice the for _ in range(10). That cap is not optional polish — it's a seatbelt. An agent that loops with no limit can spin forever, burning money and tokens (the chunks of text the model reads and writes, which you pay for).

What turns a model into an agent

Four ingredients, roughly:

  • A goal, usually set in the System Prompt (the standing instructions that frame every turn).
  • Tools — the actions it's allowed to take.
  • Memory — the running conversation, plus optionally a longer-term store so it remembers across sessions (see AI Memory Systems).
  • The loop — the engine that keeps it going.

GPS, not a printed map

Here's a second analogy. A normal program is a printed map: a fixed route written in advance. An agent is a GPS that re-plans. Hit a closed road and the GPS reroutes; it didn't know the obstacle ahead of time. That's the real power — agents handle messy, unpredictable tasks where you can't script every branch. The trade-off is that, like a GPS occasionally sending you down a farm track, agents can wander off, repeat themselves, or confidently do the wrong thing.

Agent vs chatbot vs workflow

Plain chatbotAI agent
One question, one answerA goal, many self-chosen steps
No tools or actionsPicks and runs tools in a loop
You drive every turnIt drives itself toward the goal
Predictable, cheapFlexible, costlier, can go astray

One more distinction worth holding: an agent decides its own path, while a fixed Agentic Workflow is a path you wired up in advance. Many real products are mostly fixed workflow with one small agentic step — because you only pay for unpredictability where you actually need it.

Real examples in 2026: coding assistants that read your repo, edit files, run the tests, and fix what broke; research agents that search, read, and write a cited summary; Browser Agents that click through a website to complete a booking. Bigger jobs split work across several agents — see Multi-Agent Systems.
mentalModel: An AI agent is a capable intern, not a vending machine: you hand it a goal, and it loops — think, use a tool, look at the result, decide again — until the job is done. The model is the brain; your code is the hands and the seatbelt.
mistakes: Calling a single tool call an 'agent'. One model output that triggers one function is just Tool Calling. It only becomes an agent when there's a loop that feeds results back and lets the model decide again.

No step limit or budget cap. An unbounded loop can run forever, calling tools and burning tokens (the paid text chunks). Always set a max-steps count and ideally a cost ceiling — it's a seatbelt, not a nicety.

Giving it powerful tools with no guardrails. An agent that can delete files, send emails, or spend money will eventually do so wrongly. Add confirmations for risky actions and keep dangerous tools read-only or sandboxed until you trust it.

Vague tool descriptions. The model only knows what a tool does from your text description. 'Searches stuff' leads to misuse; a precise description of inputs, outputs, and when to use it is what makes the agent pick the right action.
Letting the conversation history grow unbounded. Every step is appended, so a long-running agent can overflow its context window. You need to trim, summarise, or offload old steps to memory.

Assuming more autonomy is always better. If the task is predictable, a fixed Agentic Workflow is cheaper, faster, and far easier to debug than a free-roaming agent. Reach for an agent only when the path genuinely can't be scripted.
exercise:
Build the smallest possible agent by hand. Define two fake tools as plain Python functions: get_weather(city) (return a hard-coded number) and convert_c_to_f(c). Write the think-act-observe loop with a max_steps cap. Give it the goal "What's the weather in Tokyo in Fahrenheit?" and have it (1) call get_weather, (2) observe the Celsius result, (3) call convert_c_to_f, then (4) answer. Print every step so you can watch it reason, act, and observe.

Stretch goal: Add a third tool the goal doesn't need (e.g. get_stock_price) and confirm the agent does not call it. Then deliberately make get_weather return an error string and watch whether the agent retries, picks another approach, or gets stuck looping — then add logic to bail out gracefully after N failed steps.
solution: The shape to aim for: a loop where each pass sends the full message history to the model, checks whether the reply is a tool request or a final answer, and on a tool request runs your function and appends the result before looping again. The 'win' is seeing two tool calls happen in sequence — weather first, then conversion — with the second call using the number from the first. That hand-off is the essence of agency: the model chose step two based on what it observed in step one. For the stretch goal, a good agent ignores the irrelevant stock tool (proof your tool descriptions are clear) and, on the error, either retries once or reports the failure rather than looping uselessly. If it spins, that's exactly why the max_steps cap exists — without it you'd have an infinite, expensive loop. Logging every step is the single best debugging habit for agents: when one misbehaves, the transcript shows you the precise moment it went off the rails.
summary: An AI agent is a model wrapped in a loop: given a goal, it repeatedly thinks, calls a tool, observes the result, and decides the next step until done (the think-act-observe / ReAct pattern). The model is the brain but takes no action itself — your code runs the tools and drives the loop. Agents shine on messy, unpredictable tasks you can't fully script, but cost more and can wander, so always cap the steps, guardrail risky tools, and prefer a fixed workflow when the path is known.

keyTerms.def: A language model placed inside a loop that pursues a goal on its own by repeatedly thinking, acting with tools, and reacting to results.
The repeating cycle of think -> act (call a tool) -> observe (read the result) -> decide again, run by your code around the model.
A common agent pattern where the model alternates between reasoning about what to do and taking a concrete action, using each result to inform the next step.
An action the agent is allowed to take, exposed as a function your code can run (search, calculator, database query, send email).
How much the agent decides for itself versus following a fixed script; more autonomy means more flexibility but less predictability.
A hard limit on how many loop iterations an agent may run, preventing it from looping forever and burning tokens and money.
A safety constraint around an agent's actions, such as requiring confirmation before risky operations or restricting which tools it can use.
The result of a tool call that gets fed back into the conversation so the model can decide its next move.
